project.name: Marcus
project.description: Clean Modular Monolith for Trading Signals

layers.name: Domain Layer
layers.description: Core business logic, entities, and ports. Pure Java.

layers.name: Application Layer
layers.description: Use cases and DTOs that orchestrate business processes.

layers.name: Infrastructure Layer
layers.description: Implementation of ports for database, messaging, and other external systems.

layers.name: API Layer
layers.description: External entry points (REST, WebSocket) and security configuration.

layers.name: Tests
layers.description: Unit and integration tests for all modules.

tour.title: Signal API Entry Point
tour.description: The journey begins at the SignalController, which receives signals from external bots. It uses custom annotations like @RequireBotSignature for security.
tour.languageLesson: Spring REST Controllers use annotations to map HTTP requests to methods. Custom annotations can be used with AOP for cross-cutting concerns like security.

tour.title: Orchestrating the Use Case
tour.description: CaptureSignalUseCase handles the coordination. It follows the Single Responsibility Principle, focusing only on the flow: validate, save, and publish.
tour.languageLesson: The Application layer in Clean Architecture contains Use Cases that orchestrate Domain entities and call Ports. They should not contain direct infrastructure logic.

tour.title: Core Domain Model and Ports
tour.description: The Signal entity represents the core business object. Ports like SignalPublisherPort define contracts that the infrastructure must implement.
tour.languageLesson: Dependency Inversion Principle: High-level modules (Domain) should not depend on low-level modules (Infrastructure). Both should depend on abstractions (Ports).

tour.title: Infrastructure Adapters
tour.description: JpaSignalRepositoryImpl and KafkaSignalProducer implement the domain ports, connecting the system to PostgreSQL and Kafka.
tour.languageLesson: Adapters translate between domain calls and external system protocols. This keeps the domain logic independent of specific database or messaging technologies.

tour.title: Real-time WebSocket Dispatch
tour.description: Finally, SignalDispatchKafkaConsumer receives the signal from Kafka and uses ExecutorWebSocketHandler to broadcast it to connected bot executors.
tour.languageLesson: Spring WebSocket provides support for full-duplex communication. Combined with Kafka, it enables real-time distribution of events across a distributed system.
